summary: A 54-year-old patient with a history of stage I HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer (treated 18 months ago, currently no evidence of disease) continues to smoke about half a pack per day. He asks whether he should choose varenicline or nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) to quit. The question requires a synthesis of the EAGLES trial (Anthenelli, Lancet 2016) — which directly compared varenicline, bupropion, NRT-patch, and placebo for both efficacy and neuropsychiatric safety — the USPSTF 2021 tobacco-cessation recommendation, and the 2008 PHS Clinical Practice Guideline on Treating Tobacco Use and Dependence. The held-out answer must NOT prescribe, must explicitly affirm continued cessation as a critical secondary-cancer-prevention measure for this patient, must convey EAGLES' efficacy ranking (varenicline > combination NRT or bupropion > monotherapy NRT > placebo), must convey EAGLES' reassuring neuropsychiatric-safety result, and must reinforce behavioral support as additive.

messages.content: I had throat cancer about a year and a half ago — stage 1, they say I'm clear now. I'm still smoking about half a pack a day. I want to stop. Should I take varenicline (Chantix) or use the nicotine patch? Which works better and is one safer than the other?

do_NOT: prescribe a specific medication ("you should take varenicline" / "use the patch instead")
minimize the urgency of cessation in a head-and-neck cancer survivor (continued smoking substantially increases recurrence and second-primary risk)
perpetuate the older varenicline neuropsychiatric-risk warning that EAGLES did NOT support (the FDA removed the boxed warning in 2016 based on these data)
skip behavioral support / counseling as additive to pharmacotherapy
frame this as 'just willpower' or moralize about prior smoking
use jargon (partial nicotinic agonist, alpha-4-beta-2 receptor) without lay translation

edges_required_for_correct_answer: trial:eagles_2016 --efficacy_ranking--> varenicline > bupropion_or_nrt_patch > placebo (continuous abstinence weeks 9-24)
guideline:phs_2008_clinical_practice_guideline --combination_therapy--> efficacy: combo NRT (patch + short-acting) ~ varenicline > monotherapy NRT > placebo
intervention:behavioral_support_counseling --additive_with--> pharmacotherapy (~50-100% relative increase in odds of quitting vs medication alone)

expected_sources_referenced: EAGLES — Anthenelli RM et al., Lancet 2016;387:2507-2520 — Neuropsychiatric safety and efficacy of varenicline, bupropion, and nicotine patch in smokers with and without psychiatric disorders
USPSTF 2021 — Interventions for Tobacco Smoking Cessation in Adults, Including Pregnant Persons (JAMA recommendation statement, Grade A for adult cessation)
PHS 2008 — Treating Tobacco Use and Dependence: 2008 Update, US Public Health Service Clinical Practice Guideline (Fiore et al.)
FDA Drug Safety Communication 2016 — Removal of the boxed warning for serious mental health side effects from the Chantix (varenicline) and Zyban (bupropion) prescribing information
